name: Product discovery
goal: Move from a fuzzy problem space to a validated direction: clear user insight, prioritized opportunities, and a small set of evidence-backed bets ready for delivery planning.
description: Five-week discovery arc for a product trio or solo PM: frame the problem, learn from users, compare alternatives, size and de-risk, then hand off a crisp backlog slice. Dates are a template—shift to your kickoff.

tasks.title: Frame the problem, constraints, and success signals
tasks.content:
Discovery fails when nobody agrees what “good” means.

Document:
- Problem statement in one paragraph: who struggles, when, and why it matters now
- Business / strategy constraints: timeline, budget, compliance, platforms
- Non-goals for this discovery phase (explicitly out of scope)
- Success signals: what evidence would change your mind? (behavior, willingness to pay, retention proxy—pick what fits)

Align:
- Review with sponsor or stakeholders; capture disagreements as hypotheses, not opinions

Done when:
- A one-pager or brief exists and is linked from the team home
- You have 3–5 written hypotheses you could falsify with research

tasks.title: User research: interviews, synthesis, and insight themes
tasks.content:
Bias toward talking to real people, not only analytics.

Plan:
- Recruit 8–12 participants across segments that matter (include edge cases if risk is high)
- Semi-structured guide: context, recent behavior, workarounds, jobs-to-be-done

Run:
- Take notes or recordings with consent; debrief same day

Synthesize:
- Affinity map or tagging until themes stabilize
- Separate observation vs inference; flag contradictions

Done when:
- A short readout: top themes, quotes, surprises, and “what we still do not know”
- At least one theme directly challenges an earlier stakeholder assumption

tasks.title: Competitive and alternative analysis
tasks.content:
Users always have another option, including spreadsheets and doing nothing.

Map:
- Direct competitors and substitutes (manual processes, other tools, status quo)
- For each: core value prop, pricing model, gaps, and what users praise or hate

Implications:
- Where you can differentiate vs where you must reach parity
- Risks of copying surface features without understanding the job

Done when:
- A simple comparison table or landscape diagram the team references in discussion
- Clear statement of your intended wedge in one sentence

tasks.title: Opportunity sizing, risks, and decision workshop
tasks.content:
Turn themes into choices with explicit tradeoffs.

Sizing (pick what fits):
- TAM/SAM roughing, funnel math, or “how many users hit this pain weekly”

Risks:
- Technical, legal, adoption, cannibalization—each with a mitigation or spike plan

Workshop:
- Facilitate prioritization (impact vs confidence vs effort, or RICE, or opportunity scoring)
- Kill or park ideas that fail the success signals from week one

Done when:
- Ranked list of 3–5 opportunities with owner per next validation step
- Written record of what was deprioritized and why

tasks.title: Experiments, solution sketches, and delivery handoff
tasks.content:
Close discovery with artifacts engineering can actually use.

Experiments:
- For top bets: prototype, concierge test, pricing probe, or wizard-of-oz—whatever is cheapest for the learning goal
- Define success/fail criteria before you run them

Solution:
- Low-fi flows or storyboards aligned to prioritized opportunities
- Explicit open questions for build (data, integrations, edge cases)

Handoff:
- Slice into epics or milestones with measurable outcomes
- Joint session with engineering/design to sanity-check scope and dependencies

Done when:
- Backlog top items have acceptance notes tied to user evidence
- Discovery doc archived with links to research and experiment results
